coredata.dc:title: Redox-responsive FRET-based polymer dot with BODIPY for fluorescence imaging-guided chemotherapy of tumor
coredata.prism:publicationName: European Journal of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmaceutics
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Young Kwang
Lee, Jung Eun
Ryplida, Benny
Choi, Cheong A
Mazrad, Zihnil Adha Islamy
Lee, Gibaek
Lee, Sangkug
In, Insik
Jeong, Ji Hoon
Park, Sung Young
coredata.dc:description: Redox-responsive polymer dot (PD) were synthesized from disulfide cross-linked polymers in a carbonized process to allow quenching effects by loading of boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPY) onto the matrix. The disulfide linkage facilitated degradation of the PD system by intracellular glutathione (GSH), leading to fluorescence recovery by BODIPY and intracellular drug release. The paclitaxel release profile showed that approximately 100% of the drug escaped from the matrix in response to 10 mM GSH, whereas less than 10% was released in the absence of GSH. In vitro studies showed that quenching produced by BODIPY loading enabled visual monitoring of cancer cell death, as the quenching disappeared when BODIPY was released by GSH inside of cancer cells. The PD contain disulfide bonds representing a GSH-triggered ligand; thus, nanocarriers presented enhanced in vivo chemotherapeutic inhibition in xenograft tumor-bearing mice localized at the cancer location, guided by fluorescent off-on system tracking and measured by the release of BODIPY. This platform reacts to the redox level in sensitive manner and cancer cell death can be monitored by fluorescence, making this platform useful for bio-applications, particularly in vitro and in vivo therapy and diagnosis, while considering the cell physiological environment. This system may be useful for wider medical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer dot
Redox-responsive
Fluorescent off-on
Paclitaxel
FRET effect

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: European Journal of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmaceutics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro drug release of PD(BODIPY/PTX)
In vivo antitumor therapy and histology
In vitro GSH-dependent cell viability study

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Grant No. 10062079 from the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy ( MOTIE ) and the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF ) funded by the Ministry of Education ( NRF-2017R1A2B2002365 and 2018R1A6A1A03023788 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of tumor-triggered fluorescent on/off imaging with PTX release-guided redox-responsive matrix drug delivery system. (b) Scheme of endocytosis mechanism of PD(BODIPY/PTX) in response to GSH solution.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images, Size distribution (DLS), atomic force microscope (AFM) images, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey scan of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2s spectra (inset picture: narrow scan of S 2s spectra) of (a) PD(BODIPY/PTX) and (b) PD(BODIPY/PTX) after 10 mM GSH treatment.

Luminescence intensity of PD(BODIPY/PTX) with different concentrations of GSH at (a) 360 nm for PD and (b) 520 nm for BODIPY excitation. (c and d) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement and Ellman’s assay of PD(BODIPY/PTX) with different concentrations of GSH at 412 nm (PD concentration 1 mg/mL).
In vitro release profile of PD(BODIPY/PTX) with different concentrations of GSH in PBS at physiological and tumor pH.

(a) Cellular uptake analysis of PD(BODIPY/PTX) before and after treatment with N-ethyl maleimide (NEM, 5 μM) or thioctic acid (TA, 10 μM). (b–d) Concentration-dependent MTT assay of cell viability after treatment with different concentrations of PD(BODIPY) and PD(BODIPY/PTX) before and after treatment with NEM (5 μM) and TA (10 μM) in (b) MDCK cells, (c) MDA-MB-231 cells, and (d) HeLa cells (1 × 105 cells/mL). *Dunnett’s t test was calculated with p < 0.01, parentheses (μg): PTX concentration in PD(BODIPY/PTX).
CLSM images of PD(BODIPY/PTX) and PD(BODIPY/PTX) after treatment with 5 μM NEM and PD(BODIPY/PTX) after 10 μM TA treatment in MDA-MB-231 cells, HeLa cells, and MDCK cells (1 × 105 cells/mL).

CLSM images of MDA-MB-231 cells treated with PD(BODIPY/PTX) at different times and stained with Lysotracker and nuclear stain (red) (control blue is only PD(BODIPY/PTX) (1 mg/mL) and red is a marker for nuclear stain). The scale bar is 50 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Time-dependent in vivo imaging study of Balb/c tumor mice after injection with PD, PD(BODIPY), and PD(BODIPY/PTX). (b) In vivo pharmacokinetic profile of PD(BODIPY/PTX) in MDA-MB-231 tumor-carrying mice at different times. (c) Histological analysis of major organs isolated from mice treated with PD(BODIPY/PTX). The analysis was conducted 5 times per group. *Dunnett’s t test was conducted with p < 0.01.

head.ce:title: Redox-responsive FRET-based polymer dot with BODIPY for fluorescence imaging-guided chemotherapy of tumor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: IT Convergence Material R&D Group, Korea Institute of Industrial Technology, Cheonan-si, Chungcheongnam-do 35-3, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemical & Biological Eng., Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju-Si, Chungbuk 380-702, Republic of Korea (S.Y. Park). School of Pharmacy, Sungkyunkwan University, 300 Cheoncheon-dong, Jangan-gu, Suwon-si, Gyeonggi-do 440-746, Republic of Korea (J.H. Jeong).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Y. K. Kim and J. E. Lee contributed same to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Redox-responsive polymer dot (PD) were synthesized from disulfide cross-linked polymers in a carbonized process to allow quenching effects by loading of boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPY) onto the matrix. The disulfide linkage facilitated degradation of the PD system by intracellular glutathione (GSH), leading to fluorescence recovery by BODIPY and intracellular drug release. The paclitaxel release profile showed that approximately 100% of the drug escaped from the matrix in response to 10 mM GSH, whereas less than 10% was released in the absence of GSH. In vitro studies showed that quenching produced by BODIPY loading enabled visual monitoring of cancer cell death, as the quenching disappeared when BODIPY was released by GSH inside of cancer cells. The PD contain disulfide bonds representing a GSH-triggered ligand; thus, nanocarriers presented enhanced in vivo chemotherapeutic inhibition in xenograft tumor-bearing mice localized at the cancer location, guided by fluorescent off-on system tracking and measured by the release of BODIPY. This platform reacts to the redox level in sensitive manner and cancer cell death can be monitored by fluorescence, making this platform useful for bio-applications, particularly in vitro and in vivo therapy and diagnosis, while considering the cell physiological environment. This system may be useful for wider medical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Tumors are currently a serious problem causing large numbers of deaths worldwide; thus, many new therapeutic strategies are actively being examined. Among these strategies, multifunctional nanoparticle-based theranostics, including targeting, imaging, and therapy, have provided new opportunities for the diagnosis and treatment of cancer [1,2]. Abnormal conditions in the cancer microenvironment require more advanced strategies for selective and effective drug delivery systems [3]. Stimuli-responsive moieties have the potential to advance previous methods through functionalization under the different conditions present in normal and tumor cells [4,5]. However, it remains difficult to ensure that both active and passive targeting agents that are drug-loaded reach the appropriate organelle because conjugation with a labeling agent involves complex techniques. Previously, theranostics techniques have been used with fluorescent dyes for imaging of drug delivery systems, but these approaches are complex. Therefore, methods for effectively killing cancer cells in a safe and accurate manner using guided imaging systems must be improved.

The tumor environment is distinct from that of normal cells in terms of enzyme levels, pH, and reducing agents, which have been intensively studied as parameters for controlling drug release from nanocarriers [6]. Glutathione (GSH), an important reducing agent in cells, is present at approximately 2–5 mM in normal cells, but exhibits up to 4-fold higher concentrations in cancer cells [3,7]. GSH chemically degrades disulfide bonds via an exchange reaction with dithiol-disulfide groups [8]. Because of their stability are excellent blood circulation, the high levels of GSH in cancer cells enable the design of tunable materials containing a disulfide group as an intracellular redox-responsive drug delivery system in targeted cancer cells [8]. Over the past several decades, various polymers with disulfide linkages have been explored for effective drug delivery, but remain limited to bioimaging and controlled release systems [9–11].

Numerous studies of polymer dot (PD) have been performed such as in the electrical and optical materials fields. Their properties, such as high brightness, desirable photostability, fast emission rates, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity, suggest that PD can be used as fluorescent biomarkers in living cells [12,13]. Additionally, PD consist of abundant π-π bonds, making them suitable for aromatic/drug molecule loading through strong π-π stacking or hydrophobic interactions [14]. Thus, PD can be used as transporters without causing structural changes to the drugs. However, it remains challenging to effectively monitor and kill cancer cells under tumor-responsive environment conditions because a PD system with only fluorescent properties cannot be traced. Therefore, stimuli-responsive PD are critical factors for assessing drug delivery and therapeutic efficacy to a specific cancer cell environment without side effects [15,16].

The application of fluorescent signals alone is not suitable for tracking drug delivery because of their low sensitivity. Many recent studies have reported new models based on “switch-on/off” methods, which show high sensitivity and selectivity detection with quenching effects induced by nanomaterials such as graphene oxide, tungsten oxide, gold nanomaterial, and organic dyes through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [17–19]. Because GSH is present in cancer cells, we hypothesized that redox-responsive PD with disulfide cross-linked nanoparticles loaded with quenchers and drugs can be used for fluorescence-guided cell death. Here, we synthesized a redox-sensitive nanoparticle from a carbonized disulfide cross-linked polymer (PD) to allow for a FRET mechanism by boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPY) [PD(BODIPY)] and paclitaxel (PTX) carrier [PD (BODIPY/PTX)] through π-π stacking or hydrophobic interactions. BODIPY showed a quenching effect on the PD based on the fluorescent off/on system which was affected by the GSH concentration: after cleavage of the cross-linked disulfide PD, BODIPY was released from the core of PD, switching on fluorescent emission and regulating PTX escape for cancer chemotherapy. After subcutaneous injection in vivo, PD (BODIPY/PTX) passively accumulated at MDA-MB-231 cell sites in mice. The high level GSH promoted cleavage of the disulfide bond for qualified simultaneous PTX transport and BODIPY release to recover the fluorescence. The prepared matrix represents a smart drug nanocarrier for simultaneous diagnosis and chemotherapy to enhance drug efficacy against devastating malignant tumors using a fluorescent off/on system with triggered drug release in response to the cancer cell internal environment.

All materials were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA), Gibco BRL (Grand Island, NY, USA), and Bioneer Corp. (Daejeon, Korea). Each step in the material synthesis and characterization are described in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release of PD(BODIPY/PTX)
ce:section.ce:para: Drug release profiles were observed using a dialysis method. The details have been provided in the Supporting Information (Section 1.6). Next, we measured the amount of drug released by measuring the absorbance at 230 nm by UV-spectroscopy. PTX release was compared and calculated using the PTX calibration curve. In the next interval, new PBS was added to maintain a constant capacity.

Cell viability was examined using MTT reagent. Briefly, 200 μL of each MDA-MB-231, HeLa, and MDCK (Madin-Darby Canine Kidney) mammalian cell lines in RPMI medium (105 cells/mL) was transferred into a 96-well plate for 24 h for incubation at physiological temperature with 5% CO2. To control intracellular GSH levels, TA (GSH-enhancing factor, 10 μM) and NEM (GSH inhibitor, 5 μM) were added to each sample group. For cellular viability evaluation, the cells were treated with 0.01–1 mg/mL PD(BODIPY) and PD(BODIPY/PTX) in RPMI medium and then incubated for 24 h. In other sample groups, the cells were first incubated with TA for 24 h or NEM for 3 h before incubation in media containing PD(BODIPY) and PD(BODIPY/PTX). After replacing a volume of 180 μL with new medium, MTT reagent (5 mg/mL) in PBS was added and incubation was continued for 4 h. Next, 200 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide as a solubilizing agent was added and reaction was shaken for 15 min. Viability was evaluated by measuring the absorbance of 570 nm and determining the difference between the values of cells treated with PD(BODIPY) and PD(BODIPY/PTX) and untreated cells.

Cellular penetration of PD(BODIPY/PTX) was observed by CLSM. First, 105 MDA-MB-231 cells/mL were added to an 8-well plate and incubated for 24 h at physiological temperature with 5% CO2. For each experiment group, we added TA (10 μM) and NEM (5 μM) to modulate the intracellular GSH concentration for 30 min, and then the cells were treated with PD(BODIPY/PTX), followed by addition of 0.01 mg/mL of lysotracker or nuclei stain and incubation for 3 h. Three washes were performed using PBS to remove free PD(BODIPY/PTX). Finally, 20 μL of the final solution was plated on a cover glass and observed by CLSM at 20× magnification.

Two mammalian cell lines, MDA-MB-231 and MDCK, were co-cultured in 6-well plates at a density of 105 cells/mL per well in a 5% CO2 incubator (37 °C). The medium was replaced with PD(BODIPY/PTX) solution after 24 h and continually incubated for different times. The cells were analyzed using an Attune NxT Acoustic Focusing Cytometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) at excitation and emission wavelengths of 405 and 450/50 nm, respectively.

Model mice containing MDA-MB-231 cancerous cells (Balb/c mice) were injected with 50 μL of PD(BODIPY/PTX) followed by profile imaging using a fluorescence imaging instrument (VISQUETM, Anyang, Korea) for various times post-injection. All animal procedures were performed in accordance with the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of Sungkyunkwan University in Republic of Korea and approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antitumor therapy and histology
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 1 × 107 MDA-MB-231 tumor cells/mL was subcutaneously injected into one side of each mouse to investigate the in vivo antitumor effect of PD(BODIPY/PTX). Tumor organs were freshly harvested from the mice when the tumor size reached approximately 100 mm3. Tumor-bearing mice were subcutaneously administered 50 µL of PBS (control), PTX (1 mg/kg), or PD(BODIPY/PTX) (10 mg/kg) every 2 days on days 0, 2, 4, 6, and 8 for a total of five doses. Tumor volume was monitored to evaluate tumor growth for 25 days. Tumor capacity was determined using the equation V = (L × W2)/2, where V is volume, L is tumor length, and W is tumor width. In vivo imaging was performed using the VISQUETM imaging system after injection.

For histology analysis, the tumor tissues of PD(BODIPY/PTX)-treated tumor-bearing model mice were recovered from the sacrificed animals. Next, the tissue samples were treated with 10% neutral-buffered formalin and embedded in paraffin. To evaluate in vivo cytotoxicity effects, 4-µm tissue slices were cleaved and stained with hematoxylin and eosin.

The principles of the synthesis and cancer therapy as well as detection of the prepared materials are shown in Fig. 1 . Polymer dot (PD) were synthesized by treating the disulfide crosslinked-polymer with sulfuric acid (H2SO4) as an oxidative reagent to prepare a highly photoluminescent nanomaterial that can function as a redox-responsive matrix and hydrophobic drug carrier and bioimaging material. The carbonization methods using the oxidative reagent rely on the polymer as a carbon source and dehydration of the polymer structure by sulfuric acid, which increased the CC bonds of PD [20,21]. The PD emitted high fluorescence intensity; after BODIPY loading, the fluorescence signal of PD suddenly decreased because of the overlap in the fluorescence nanomaterial excitation wavelength and absorption wavelength of BODIPY. Here, PD(BODIPY) underwent a FRET effect because of energy transfer between the PD (donor) to BODIPY (acceptor) to result in fluorescent quenching [19]. Moreover, the disulfide cross-linked fluorescent matrix plays a role in the FRET quenching effect of loading BODIPY into the π-π bond localized PD to produce a fluorescence on/off system. PTX was applied in the PD system as a model drug to investigate tumor-triggered delivery systems [PD(BODIPY/PTX)]. Generally, disulfide cross-linked PD are hydrophobic and contain π–π bonds which enabled BODIPY and PTX loading via hydrophobic interactions. At high concentrations of GSH, the cross-linked disulfide was cleaved, decreasing the hydrophobicity and disrupting the hydrophobic interactions between the drug (BODIPY and PTX) and PD system, resulting in release behavior.

Fig. S1(a) shows the results of structural analysis of each synthesis step by 1H-nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. The 1H NMR spectra revealed a peak at 7.2–8 ppm, confirming an aromatic proton of the BODIPY group in PD(BODIPY) [22]. After adding PTX to PD(BODIPY), peaks for PTX were detected at 7.2–8 ppm along with peaks for BODIPY as shown in the control pure BODIPY and PTX (Fig. S2, Supporting Information) [23]. In 10 samples treated with mM GSH, the loaded BODIPY and PTX were released because of cleavage of the disulfide bonds, resulting in disappearance of the BODIPY and PTX peaks. The 13C NMR spectra for PD and PD(BODIPY/PTX) are shown in Fig. S3. In the 13C NMR of the PD(BODIPY/PTX) sample, the peaks at 40–50 ppm were retained, confirming formation of a carbon framework and the fused carbon ring of PD attached to electron-withdrawing atoms such as oxygen or nitrogen. Additionally, the 13C NMR data showed a small peak at ∼163 ppm, indicating a carbonyl group, such as amide, ester, and acid moieties produced by carbonization, while PTX and BODIPY loading was confirmed by the peak at ∼132 ppm [24]. As shown in Fig. S1(b), the wide absorbance in the 300–400-nm region confirmed the presence of π-π bonding in the PD system. Additionally, new intense peaks at 512 nm appeared for PD(BODIPY), confirming the absorption properties of BODIPY inside the PD system. In the UV–Vis spectra of PD(BODIPY/PTX), we observed a peak at 230 nm, which represents PTX absorption. This data confirmed the successful loading of BODIPY and PTX in the core of PD. To investigate disulfide bond cleavage performance in response to GSH, we treated PD(BODIPY/PTX) with GSH. The optical characteristics at 512 and 230 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum disappeared, indicating the release of BODIPY and PTX from the core of the PD because of cleavage of the disulfide bonds in PD. Further investigation about the successful loading of BODIPY and PTX was conducted using FT-IR in Fig. S1(c). After BODIPY and PTX loading, the broad band at 1678 cm−1 was observed indicated C-N stretching of the triazole ring of BODIPY [50]. Moreover, additional peaks were observed at 1521 cm−1 which is specific to PTX aromatic CC stretching and at 2935 cm−1 due to CH stretching vibration [51]. In the presence of GSH, BODIPY and PTX peaks were disappeared marked the release of BODIPY and PTX from PD matrix. Additionally, zeta potential of before and after BODIPY loading changed from −15.36 mV to −8.73 mV (Fig. S1(d)) verified the successful loading of BODIPY [52]. On the other hand, when PTX was loaded into PD(BODIPY) matrices, the zeta potential suddenly decreased to −26.13 mV, suggesting the successful loading of PTX [53].

From endocytosis to drug release in the drug delivery process, the size of the nanocarrier serves an important role in controllable in vitro and in vivo drug delivery. TEM images and DLS data showed that the particle size of PD(BODIPY/PTX) was 190.5, which was larger than that of PD alone and PD(BODIPY) which are 145.7 nm and 162.9 nm, respectively (Fig. S4, Supporting Information). The TEM lattice spacing was 0.235 nm, confirming that this matrix had a typical graphene lattice after carbonization. As shown in Fig. 2 (b), the particle size of PD(BODIPY/PTX) was reduced from 190.5 to 26 nm after 10 mM GSH treatment because of disulfide bond cleavage [25,26]. Further analysis of the aqueous stability of PD would be advantageous for maximizing therapeutic efficiency [5]. In cell media, BODIPY was precipitated in cell media resulting in low absorbance spectra at 520 nm. However, the BODIPY and PTX absorbance when loaded in PD(BODIPY/PTX) showed stable signal for 60 h and the colloidal particle size remain unchanged in cell media, as shown in Fig. S12. Moreover, the AFM images of PD (Fig. S4(b1)) revealed a particle depth of 12.5 nm; after loading of BODIPY and PTX, their sizes were increased to 21.3 and 23.7 nm, respectively, together with an increase in particle size. After GSH treatment, the AFM images showed the redox-responsive behavior of this matrix as an 8.5-nm decrease in depth, which was lower than that of PD alone. The result can be explained by cleavage of the disulfide bonds induced by GSH reduction activity and release of BODIPY and PTX [27]. Further evidence of the chemical structure of our smart matrix was provided by the XPS binding energy spectrum shown in Fig. 2. The binding energies of each substance (C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p) described the binding energies of each element in PD(BODIPY/PTX). In the spectrum of S 2p, shown in the inset in Fig. 2(a and b), the intensities of thiol (162.4 eV) and disulfide (164.1 eV) were compared. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the intensity of the disulfide bond was confirmed to be higher than that of the thiol bond, whereas the opposite result was observed after treatment with 10 mM GSH (Fig. 2(b)). The XPS atomic concentration of sulfur before GSH treatment was approximately 1.3% and remain unchanged after GSH treatment. Moreover, Fig. S5 shows the XPS narrow scan C 1s of PD(BODIPY/PTX) and GSH-treated PD(BODIPY/PTX), which confirmed that the intensity of CC (283.8 eV) decreased relative to the intensity of CC (284.9 eV) after the release of BODIPY and PTX by GSH. These results demonstrate that PD(BODIPY/PTX) cross-linked by disulfide bonds was cleaved by GSH, causing the thiol bond to release the loaded BODIPY and PTX.

Fig. S6(a and b) shows the fluorescence quenching effect according to the amount of loaded BODIPY as a quencher. The results shown in Figs. S6(a) and S7 reveal that the concentration of quencher influenced the fluorescence intensity of the PD; an increased quencher concentration decreased the fluorescence intensity of the PD and vice versa. As a result, a ratio of 100:1 for the PD and BODIPY was suitable for further analysis. The graph of the ratio of initial fluorescence (F0) to final fluorescence (F) (F0/F) followed the Stern–Volmer equation, suggesting a quenching mechanism [28,29]. According to the concentration of quencher, the quenching mechanism between PD and BODIPY was a donor and acceptor process that occurred through a FRET phenomenon. Decay time measurements confirmed that the initial decay time τ0 = 0.488 ns decreased to τ1 = 0.75 ns as the concentration of quencher was increased, as shown in Fig. S6(c) (Supporting Information), corresponding to FRET quenching. Furthermore, the Stern–Volmer equation can be used to reveal the mechanism more accurately by measuring the decay time through the FRET process. As shown in the inset of Fig. S6(b), the decreasing decay time of PD(BODIPY) was related to the quencher concentration, supporting that F0/F = τ0/τ [24].

Disulfide bonds can respond to high levels of GSH in cancer cells through a reduction-oxidation mechanism, resulting in cleavage of the disulfide into a thiol bond and release of the drug by the nanocarrier. As shown in Fig. S8(a), the PD system showed no quenching effect in the absence of GSH at 0 min. However, the fluorescence intensity was slightly increased over time and PD size decreased as a function of the GSH response (Fig. S8(b)). Furthermore, we determined the time dependence of PL intensity and particle size of PD(BODIPY/PTX) according to the GSH concentration. Fig. 3 (a and b) shows that the fluorescence intensity of PD(BODIPY/PTX) increased with increasing GSH concentration over time at excitation wavelengths of 360 (PD) and 520 nm (BODIPY) compared to 0 mM and 0 min treatment, which contrasted the fluorescence-off and was unchanged compared to the behavior of PD alone (Fig. S8, Supporting Information). Simultaneously, the particle size decreased over time, as shown in Fig. 3(c), confirming that a higher GSH concentration disrupted more disulfide bonds to generate smaller particle sizes. Furthermore, Fig. 3(d) shows the quantification the SH bonds produced by cleavage of the disulfide bonds using Ellman's assay [8,30]. To avoid GSH inhibition during the assay, we dialyzed (molecular cut-off, Mw 1000 Da) the PD(BODIPY/PTX) against deionized water prior to the assay. Moreover, the degree of substitution by sulfur in the PD quantified using Ellman’s assay was measured as 7 units. In the case of PD(BODIPY/PTX), the concentration of SH increased to 0.056 mM after 24 h in the presence of 10 mM of GSH solution as shown in Fig. S8(c). This indicates that most disulfide cleavage occurred within 3 h of addition of GSH, which was reflected in the changes in fluorescence intensity, particle size, and SH bond concentration.

UV–vis spectroscopy was used to determined the amount of BODIPY and PTX loaded in the matrices. The BODIPY loading content and BODIPY loading efficiency were 0.94% and 95.0%, respectively. The PTX loading content and PTX loading efficiency were 3.1% and 91.0%, respectively. The successful loading of PTX in the drug delivery system was determined by hydrophobic interaction and π–π stacking between PTX and PD [14]. However, BODIPY which have affinity toward hydrophobicity promoted inhibition of the hydrophobic interaction between PTX and PD in the PD(BODIPY/PTX) system. Therefore, by adjusting the loading content of BODIPY, optimized condition was achieved which affected the performance of the PD(BODIPY/PTX). For instance, PTX and BODIPY released from the nanocarriers were conducted by UV–VIS spectroscopy analysis at an absorbance value of 230 nm and 520 nm, respectively over time. As shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S12(f), we investigated the release behaviors at different GSH concentrations in different pH to examine the release profiles of PTX and BODIPY from PD(BODIPY/PTX). In the absence of GSH, less than 5% of PTX was released, even after 36 h, suggesting no breakage of disulfide crosslinking of the PD and maintain stable hydrophobic interaction between PD, BODIPY, and PTX [54–56]. As another control, we investigated PTX release under normal cell conditions (pH 7.4 and 0 mM GSH), which showed no obvious difference from PTX release under pH 6.8, indicating that pH has no specific effect on this matrix carrier. In contrast, in the presence of 10 mM GSH, up to 80% of PTX was released after 12 h, which increased to over 90% after 36 h. In similar manner, less than 5% of BODIPY was released from PD(BODIPY/PTX) matrices when the concentration of GSH is 0 mM. However, the BODIPY undergo burst release when the GSH concentration increase to 10 mM which showed the highest amount of dyes release. Moreover, we examined matrix performance at 3 and 6 mM of GSH. As a result, PD(BODIPY/PTX) showed an increasing dyes and drug release profile at high GSH concentrations because the number of cleaved disulfide bonds increased with GSH concentration. This result demonstrates the acceleration of dyes and drug release from the matrix because of cleavage of redox-sensitive disulfide bonds by GSH activity [31,32]. For better clarity of PD(BODIPY/PTX) system, fluorescence reversibility was analyzed. Initially, the PD(BODIPY/PTX) exhibited low fluorescence intensity in the absence of GSH due to quenching effect by BODIPY as shown in Fig. S14. When the concentration of GSH started to elevated to 10 mM, the fluorescence intensity suddenly increase and emitted blue fluorescence which remain unchange after 3 cycles. According to this phenomena, we concluded that the fluorescence system of PD(BODIPY/PTX) was irreversible due to the cleavage of disulfide bond on the first GSH treatment which release all of fluorescence quencher (BODIPY).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro GSH-dependent cell viability study
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the performance of PD(BODIPY/PTX) in mammalian cells, the amount GSH in the cells was adjusted using NEM, a GSH scavenger, and TA, a GSH synthesis enhancer [33]. NEM and TA affected the amount of GSH in the cell able to react with PD(BODIPY/PTX). Fig. 5 (a) shows the cellular uptake of PD(BODIPY/PTX) by the MDA-MB-231, MDCK, and HeLa cell lines. For cellular uptake, the results were MDCK [untreated (17.5%), NEM (17.3%), TA (17.8%)], HeLa [untreated (18.0%), NEM (17.4%), TA (17.6%)], and MDA-MB-231 [untreated (17.8%), NEM (17.6%), TA (17.5%)], which showed no significant differences from PD alone (Fig. S13(a)). The viability of MDCK, HeLa, and MDA-MB-231 cells after PD(BODIPY) and PD(BODIPY/PTX) treatment, as determined in MTT assays, is shown in Fig. 5(b–d). Using MDCK, MDA-MB 231, and HeLa cells alone as controls, the cell viability after PD(BODIPY) treatment remained up to 95%, indicating biocompatibility. In the case of PD(BODIPY/PTX), the degradation of disulfide at high GSH concentrations in TA-treated cells caused the release of PTX, resulting in high toxicity of all cells types. In contrast, NEM-treated cells showed relatively high viability because little PTX was released because of the low GSH concentration in the cells. Additionally, the cytotoxicity effect of treated cells in the presence of the GSH enhancer TA (MDCK (93.08%) for HeLa (35.13%) and MDA-MB-231 (38.79%)) cells was largely different from the viability of untreated cells and those treated with NEM. This may be because of the very small amount of PTX released when GSH levels in MDCK cells were low (0.2 mM), whereas GSH was higher in HeLa (26 mM) and MDA-MB-231 (20 mM) cells [34,35]. The viabilities of the different cell types confirmed the effectiveness of our anticancer drug delivery system, as the system selectively released the loaded drugs into cancer cells in a redox environment, enabling higher selectivity and improved drug delivery through passive targeted delivery [36,37]. To verify the biocompatibility, a WST-1 assay was conducted. Fig. S13(c) shows that PD(BODIPY) had desirable cell viability, in contrast to PD(BODIPY/PTX) which showed excellent biocompatibility with normal cells but high toxicity towards MDAMB and HeLa cells [38], To further validate that PD(BODIPY/PTX) can specifically kill cancer cells rather than normal cells, we used normal MDCK and cancer MDA-MB-231 cells at different ratios with co-culture techniques and observed the cells by flow cytometry and MTT assay. Fig. S9(a) shows that MDCK:MDAMB (9:1) cell viability was nearly 96%. The higher density of MDA-MB-231 cells in the co-culture dramatically lowered cell viability, indicating the selective killing of cancer cells by PD(BODIPY/PTX). The cellular uptake of MDCK:MDAMB co-culture showed similar patterns at all ratios, as shown in Fig. S9(b). MDCK:MDAMB (10:0) treated with PD(BODIPY/PTX) demonstrated low fluorescence intensity due to low concentration of GSH in contrast with MDCK:MDAMB (0:10) which emitted high fluorescence intensity as displayed in Fig. S9(c). In co-culture cells, higher fluorescence intensity was observed for high MDCK:MDAMB (1:9) compared to at lower ratios (9:1), which explained the sensitivity of PD(BODIPY/PTX) to GSH concentration.

For advanced biomedical applications, a visible color change in cells is useful for indicating the correct mechanism [39–41]. To visualize the signal effect of PD(BODIPY/PTX) in response to the GSH level, samples were examined by confocal microscopy after adding 1 mg/mL GSH to PD(BODIPY/PTX) solution for a predetermined period (Fig. S10, Supporting Information). There was nearly no fluorescence at 0 h because of fluorescence quenching by BODIPY and because the GSH was absent, no disulfide cleavage and fluorescent recovery was triggered. After 12 h, confocal microscopy confirmed the release of PTX and BODIPY from the core of the PD by showing blue and green emission, respectively. Fig. 6 displays CLSM images of MDA-MB-231, MDCK, and HeLa mammalian cells after 12 h incubation and in the presence of NEM/TA solution to adjust the GSH level. In untreated MDAMB and HeLa cells, as BODIPY was released, the quenching effect of the PD disappeared and thus fluorescence was emitted. After NEM treatment, all cell types showed quenching effects by BODIPY as a result of GSH inhibition. However, TA-treated cells emitted high fluorescence in all cells, demonstrating the recovery of fluorescence because of BODIPY release as the GSH concentration was increased by TA, even in normal MDCK cells. Furthermore, TA-treated cells showed stronger fluorescent intensities than untreated cells, suggesting that a higher concentration of GSH was produced in TA-treated cells compared to in untreated cells [33].

We synthesized a material capable of releasing PTX sensitively in response to GSH levels in the cancer environment. To explore the PTX delivery pathway, internalization of PD(BODIPY/PTX) was investigated by Lysotracker and SYTO 61 staining. The merged signal between Lysotracker and PD(BODIPY/PTX) demonstrated internalization by endocytosis and PD accumulation in the lysosomal compartment. Additionally, the overlap in the fluorescence signal of PD(BODIPY/PTX) and SYTO 61 indicated nuclear localization of PTX released from the matrices in response to the reducing environment [42,43]. Although PD(BODIPY/PTX) initially showed weak fluorescence, after 2 h the fluorescence intensity gradually increased as quenching of the accumulated BODIPY disappeared because of GSH in the cancer cells (Fig. 7 ). However, because the internalization induced by endocytosis escaped from the vesicle into the cytoplasm after 4 h, the fluorescence signals in the cell were minimized between 4 and 6 h. Fig. S11 (Supporting Information) confirms the effects of PTX released by GSH in the cells, showing fluorescence images of red and green colors representing dead cells and live cells, respectively. This result agrees with the cell viability according to the treatment of each cell, as shown by the MTT assay and confocal microscopy [44,45].

Prior to in vivo experiments, we conducted cancer cell screening to examine which kind of cancer cell that will be suitable for test. As a result, MDA-MB-231 was selected for tumor therapeutic performance with PD(BODIPY/PTX). Furthermore, hemolytic test of PD(BODIPY/PTX) was performed which showed no significant hemolysis effect towards the hemoglobin in red blood cells (Fig. S13(b)) [46,47]. The tissue-targeting capacities of PD(BODIPY) and PD(BODIPY/PTX) were evaluated using an NIRF imaging system by administering PD(BODIPY/PTX) into infected-mice (10 mg/kg body weight) [45]. Moreover, the in vivo distribution of the nanocarrier was observed at 30 min, 1, 3, 6, and 12 h after injection, as shown in Fig. 8 (a). Firstly, PD-treated tumor-bearing mice started to emit green fluorescent intensity in the first 30 h which remained unchanged until 1 h and decreased gradually until 12 h. On the other hand, at 30 min after injection, PD(BODIPY)-treated tumor-bearing mice showed fluorescent restoration of PD(BODIPY) in the cancer environment because of disulfide bond dissociation, which induced BODIPY release and reduced the FRET effect. Additionally, PD(BODIPY/PTX) emitted stronger fluorescence signals in the tumor region compared to in normal regions, indicating notable tumor internalization, and GSH affected PD(BODIPY/PTX), allowing for fluorescence recovery. Both PD(BODIPY) and PD(BODIPY/PTX) showed stable fluorescent signals during first 3 h which slowly decreased until 12 h in the tumor region because of metabolic excretion [48] . We then investigated the antitumor efficacy of PD(BODIPY/PTX) in a tumor xenograft model. As shown in Fig. 8(b), PD(BODIPY/PTX) prominently inhibited tumor growth compared to PBS and PTX alone after successive administration into tumor-bearing mice. The tumor size at 25 days post-injection decreased to 195 mm3 for PD(BODIPY/PTX) and increased to 410 mm3 after treatment with PTX alone. Simultaneously, the body weight of tumor-bearing mice was measured during the treatment with PBS, PTX, and PD(BODIPY/PTX). After injection, the body weight of mice which was injected with PBS and PTX increase in for 25 days as the tumor volume expanded (Fig. S15). However, PD(BODIPY/PTX)-treated mice showed no noticeable body-weight increase for the interval of time remarking the therapeutic capabilities of the drug delivery system. Importantly, the notable growth inhibition by PD(BODIPY/PTX) indicates that this system can prevent the unwanted side effects of PTX administration alone, suggesting that efficient intracellular delivery and GSH-triggered PTX release are important for enhanced antitumor activity. Moreover, we evaluated in vivo biocompatibility by histological analysis with hematoxylin and eosin staining, which revealed that the liver, spleen, heart, lung, and kidney had similar microscopic profiles in both control and treated organs, as shown in Fig. 8(c), without any sign of vacuolation. However, the cancer organ after treatment with PD(BODIPY/PTX) showed shrinkage of tumor cells, cell separation, and necrotic tissue compared to in controls [49]. Finally, because PD(BODIPY/PTX) sensitively reacts to the redox environment of cancer cells, our designed material can simultaneously treat and diagnose cancer via fluorescence imaging and deliver drugs to improve the therapeutic efficacy of chemotherapy with minimal side effects.

We developed a smart drug delivery matrix based on redox-sensitive PD to diagnose and treat cancer, which can prevent the premature release of drugs loaded inside the matrix when injected into blood vessels. Moreover, the matrix delivered the drugs selectively and safely in response to the high GSH level inside cancer cells via a redox-sensitive bond, allowing for selective killing of cancer cells. Furthermore, we demonstrated excellent agreement between the FRET process and cell death by loading the PD with BODIPY to visualize drug release monitoring by the fluorescence signal in response to the GSH inside the cancer cells. Furthermore, advanced real-time investigations allowed for direct kinetic monitoring of drug release. Our in vivo model also showed GSH-triggered ligand properties by enhancing chemotherapeutic inhibition in xenograft MDA-MB-231 tumor-bearing mice, which was internalized at the tumor site. Furthermore, the fluorescent on-off system can be used to track the accumulation of the PTX nanocarrier in mice in response to the GSH level. These in vitro and in vivo profiles revealed the distinction between normal and cancer cell in terms of their GSH levels, enabling the development of a matrix that can be used as a selective nanocarrier to kill cancer cells. In conclusion, we developed a smart drug delivery system that can simultaneously diagnose and treat cancer for advanced biomedical applications.
The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Grant No. 10062079 from the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) and the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2017R1A2B2002365 and 2018R1A6A1A03023788).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejpb.2018.09.025.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
